title: Probabilities of Independent and Dependent Events

summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

A bag has 5 red and 3 blue marbles. You draw one marble, replace it, then draw again. What is P(red, then blue)?

With replacement: P(A then B) = P(A) × P(B) since events are independent. Without replacement: probabilities change after each draw. Multiply the individual probabilities. The answer is 15/64. The other options — '15/56', '8/64', and '3/8' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

A bag has 4 green and 6 yellow marbles. You draw two marbles without replacement. What is P(green, then green)?

With replacement: P(A then B) = P(A) × P(B) since events are independent. Without replacement: probabilities change after each draw. Multiply the individual probabilities. The answer is 12/90 (or 2/15). The other options — '16/100', '4/25', and '6/90' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: Drawing cards without replacement is an example of:

P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B) Dependent Events Two events are dependent if the outcome of one affects the other. Independent Events Two events are independent if the outcome of one does not affect the other. The other options — 'Independent events', 'Mutually exclusive events', and 'Complementary events' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.
